data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

facade.hypothesis: The user prefers clean, understated data visualization over colorful or complex graphics
facade.label: Minimal line charts
facade.acceptImplies: Lean toward restrained, line-based aesthetics; avoid filled areas and heavy color palettes
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer richer visual language—filled shapes, color coding, or more decorative elements

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from aggregated weekly snapshots over daily data points, reducing cognitive load while maintaining trend visibility
facade.label: Weekly summary cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values temporal compression and pattern-spotting over granular daily detail; prefers summarized metrics in card format
facade.rejectImplies: User wants daily granularity, time-series depth, or data table format; finds weekly abstractions too coarse for tracking experiments

evidence.hypothesis: The user prefers clean, understated data visualization over colorful or complex graphics
evidence.implication: Lean toward restrained, line-based aesthetics; avoid filled areas and heavy color palettes

facade.hypothesis: Users building personal health dashboards want conversational, warm language that feels like a supportive coach rather than clinical software.
facade.axisTargeted: voice and personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Friendly tone with personality
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into colloquial language, encouraging tone, casual phrasing like 'Nice work!' or 'Let's see what's next' rather than 'Data recorded' or 'Next input'
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer neutral, straightforward, professional language that feels trustworthy and objective without personality — more like a medical device than a friend

facade.hypothesis: For health tracking dashboards, gradual animated reveals of data points as users scroll create a sense of discovery and reduce cognitive load, whereas instant loading feels clinical and overwhelming.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — animated progressive disclosure vs instant display
facade.label: Smooth scroll reveal
facade.acceptImplies: User wants metrics to unfold naturally; values rhythm and pacing in data consumption; may prefer dashboard to feel alive and exploratory rather than utilitarian.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers immediate full visibility; values scanning speed and direct access over aesthetic flow; dashboard should feel efficient and scannable, not choreographed.

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer gentle, approachable visual language over clinical precision
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Favor organic, forgiving forms; soften data presentation; use curves in buttons, cards, and containers
facade.rejectImplies: Lean into crisp edges and geometric clarity; use sharp grids and angular UI elements; signal precision through visual sharpness

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar for navigation and filtering, with experiment data displayed as individual cards in the main area, supports quick scanning and comparison of multiple health experiments without losing navigation context.
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values stable navigation anchors and expects to browse experiments fluidly while keeping filters/sections accessible; suits exploratory, multi-experiment workflows.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers full-width content and tab-based or full-page navigation; may indicate preference for focused single-experiment view or less visual hierarchy complexity.

facade.hypothesis: The user wants immediate celebratory feedback (badges, animations, positive reinforcement) when hitting health milestones, rather than a calm, understated tracking environment
facade.axisTargeted: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
facade.label: Confetti on wins
facade.acceptImplies: Design includes reward moments—animations, progress badges, encouraging messages when goals are hit; tracking feels like a game with momentum and celebration built in
facade.rejectImplies: Design stays quiet and neutral; progress is tracked with serene, minimal feedback; no gamification; user prefers contemplative, judgment-free monitoring over celebratory cues

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
